items.q: What caused the First World War?
items.correct: Long-term tensions (militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism) combined with the July Crisis triggered by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand.
items.misconception: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand caused the war.
items.random_wrong: Germany declared war on Britain to seize colonies.
items.why: The assassination was the trigger, not the cause. Students treat the spark as sufficient explanation, ignoring the systemic pressures (MAIN factors) that made a wider war possible.

items.q: Why did the Gunpowder Plotters try to blow up Parliament in 1605?
items.correct: Catholic gentlemen, frustrated that James I had not eased anti-Catholic laws as hoped, planned to kill the king and Lords and put a Catholic monarch on the throne.
items.misconception: Guy Fawkes planned and led the Gunpowder Plot.
items.random_wrong: The plot aimed to make England independent from the Pope.
items.why: The leader was Robert Catesby; Fawkes was the explosives expert. Students remember Fawkes because he was caught, but he was not the mastermind.

items.q: Why did the American colonies rebel against Britain?
items.correct: Disputes over taxation without representation after the Seven Years' War, growing colonial identity, and British attempts to tighten imperial control on previously autonomous colonies.
items.misconception: The colonists rebelled because they were heavily oppressed and impoverished.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain banned them from owning land west of the Atlantic.
items.why: Americans were actually among the wealthiest and least-taxed people in the empire. The revolt was about principle and political control, not material suffering — a key counter-intuitive point.

items.q: Which article of the Treaty of Versailles is known as the 'War Guilt Clause'?
items.why: It's Article 231. “Article 14” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which leader did NOT belong to the 'Big Four' at Paris in 1919?
items.why: Correct answer: Lenin. “Wilson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: To what figure was the German army limited under the Treaty of Versailles?
items.why: Exact answer: 100000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
